title: Locked vs Adaptive AI Systems
concept:
AI systems can be categorised by whether their model parameters change after deployment.

**Locked AI Systems**: Model weights are frozen after training and deployment. The system behaves deterministically for the same input at any point in time. Testing characteristics:
- Regression test suites remain valid over time — the tested state persists
- Static model cards accurately describe the deployed model indefinitely
- Easier to certify for safety-critical applications
- Performance may degrade as data distributions shift (data/concept drift), requiring manual retraining cycles

**Adaptive AI Systems**: The model continues learning from new data after deployment, updating its weights in response to new observations. Testing characteristics:
- The tested system state diverges from the deployed system state over time
- Regression suites must be continuously maintained and re-executed
- Model documentation must be updated frequently
- Can provide improved performance on new patterns without explicit retraining cycles
- Higher risk of unexpected behaviour emerging post-deployment
- Require continuous monitoring and drift detection

The choice between locked and adaptive involves a fundamental **safety vs adaptability trade-off**. High-stakes applications (medical devices, safety systems) favour locked models with infrequent, carefully controlled update cycles. Consumer-facing applications (recommendation systems, fraud detection) may benefit from adaptive models with strong monitoring guardrails.
keyPoints: Locked AI systems have frozen weights post-deployment — the tested state persists, making regression suites and model documentation stable.
Adaptive AI systems continue learning after deployment — the tested state diverges over time, requiring continuous test suite maintenance and monitoring.
Safety-critical applications favour locked models; adaptive models offer better performance on new patterns but introduce higher ongoing testing burden.

quiz.question: A medical imaging AI is deployed as a locked model. Six months later the development team discovers it now underperforms on images from a new scanner model purchased by the hospital. What is the most appropriate course of action?
options: Enable adaptive learning so the model can automatically adjust to the new scanner's output.
The locked model cannot be updated — the hospital must continue using the old scanner.
Trigger a controlled retraining cycle using images from the new scanner, re-run all safety tests, and release an updated locked model version.
Add new scanner images directly to the production database without retraining.
quiz.explanation: When a locked model underperforms on new data distributions (data drift), the correct approach is a controlled retraining cycle followed by full safety re-evaluation before deploying an updated locked model. Enabling adaptive learning would undermine the safety properties that make locked models appropriate for medical use. The hospital must not continue with a known underperforming model for patient safety reasons.
